Curriculum → Application architecture and APIs → Monoliths vs. microservices

Lesson 2 of 5

Monoliths vs. microservices

Choose decomposition based on coordination cost, deployment independence, and team boundaries rather than trend pressure.

25-35 min Application architecture and APIs 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Many interview answers become stronger when you can justify why a simple modular monolith is enough for an early phase and what pressures justify later extraction.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. What a monolith does well One deployment unit speeds early iteration2. What microservices buy you Independent scaling helps uneven workloads3. Migration pressure signals Separate hot paths or data ownership first

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Many interview answers become stronger when you can justify why a simple modular monolith is enough for an early phase and what pressures justify later extraction. 2. What a monolith does well One deployment unit speeds early iteration3. What microservices buy you Independent scaling helps uneven workloads4. Migration pressure signals Separate hot paths or data ownership first
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Many interview answers become stronger when you can justify why a simple modular monolith is enough for an early phase and what pressures justify later extraction.

What to say first

  • Show that a modular monolith is often a valid starting point.
  • Extract services for a specific reason, not fashion.
  • Call out data ownership and operational overhead.
2

Study section

What a monolith does well

A modular monolith makes local reasoning, transactions, and debugging simpler when one team owns the product.

Call these points out explicitly

  • One deployment unit speeds early iteration
  • In-process calls avoid network overhead
  • Shared code is easy but can hide poor boundaries
3

Study section

What microservices buy you

Service extraction helps when domains need independent scaling, deployment pace, language/runtime freedom, or stronger ownership boundaries.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Independent scaling helps uneven workloads
  • Failures can be isolated if dependencies are controlled
  • Operational overhead rises quickly with service count
4

Study section

Migration pressure signals

Use concrete signals such as deploy contention, divergent scaling needs, or ownership conflict to justify decomposition.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Separate hot paths or data ownership first
  • Create clear contracts before extracting data
  • Plan observability and developer tooling before multiplying services

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Show that a modular monolith is often a valid starting point.
  • Extract services for a specific reason, not fashion.
  • Call out data ownership and operational overhead.
  • Mention migration steps rather than jumping from one extreme to another.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Equating microservices with automatic scalability.
  • Splitting services before the domain is understood.
  • Ignoring testing, local development, and observability costs.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What is the first signal that a monolith should be split?
  • How would you extract one service safely from a monolith?
  • When is a monolith still the better design even at scale?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Application layer responsibilities Application architecture and APIsService discovery and coordination Application architecture and APIs

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Monoliths vs. microservices

Compare synchronous service hops against queued fan-out so you can explain where coupling shows up in production. Apply it to monoliths vs. microservices so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Use this lab to turn "Monoliths vs. microservices" into a measurable system instead of a static definition.Pressure the path around what a monolith does well and watch how it interacts with what microservices buy you.Many interview answers become stronger when you can justify why a simple modular monolith is enough for an early phase and what pressures justify later extraction.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • sync p95
  • discovery load
  • primary write pressure
  • realtime latency

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 1: edge · 100000 rps · 4 ms
  • Line 2: service · 60000 rps · 7 ms
  • Line 3: service · 26000 rps · 13 ms
  • Line 4: service · 32000 rps · 9 ms
  • Line 5: database · 9000 rps · 20 ms
  • Line 6: queue · 36000 rps · 10 ms

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • Show that a modular monolith is often a valid starting point.
  • Extract services for a specific reason, not fashion.
  • Call out data ownership and operational overhead.

Interactive diagram

Compiled simulation topology

Rendering diagram…

This preview is generated from the current flow-graph input.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • Start with the critical path from What a monolith does well and use the metrics to explain where latency or capacity moves.
  • Use the simulator to justify checklist items like Show that a modular monolith is often a valid starting point. before you propose scaling changes.
  • Treat the async queue and worker path as the escape hatch for ideas from What microservices buy you.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

0/3 prompts saved Prompt 1 of 3

Prompt flow

What you will practice

interview

Explain the concept

Not saved yet

Teach "Monoliths vs. microservices" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal for this step

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Use these guardrails

  • What is the first signal that a monolith should be split?
  • What a monolith does well
  • What microservices buy you
  • Show that a modular monolith is often a valid starting point.

Suggested structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
